messages.content: std::map use insert to insert std::make_pair , second element is the Parent class instance, this Parent class takes std::string and int as argument for its constructor.

`    myMap.insert(std::make_pair(1, Parent(“John”, 30)));
` why i do this and got error `conversion of 1st argument std::make_pair(....) is ill-formed: cannot convert rvalue of type pair<...> to parameter type ...`
why operator=(Parent& other) don't make class Parent copy-constructible?  why operator=(const Parent& other) do?

In C++, a class is copy-constructible if it has a copy constructor, and it is assignable if it has an assignment operator (`operator=`). 

The `operator=` is used for assigning values of one instance to another instance of the same class. In the case of `operator=(Parent& other)`, the function takes a non-const reference to `other`, which means that the function modifies the original instance of `other`. This function is commonly used for move semantics and should not be used for copy operations. 

On the other hand, `operator=(const Parent& other)` takes a const reference to `other`, which means that it does not modify `other`. This function is used for regular copy operations where the original value of `other` is preserved. Therefore, it is the `operator=(const Parent& other)` that makes the `Parent` class assignable and helps to create a new instance of `Parent` using an existing one.

In summary, `operator=(const Parent& other)` creates a new instance of `Parent` using an existing one without modifying the original instance, which makes it suitable for copy operations. While `operator=(Parent& other)` is typically used for move semantics or for modifying the original instance, which makes it less suitable for copy operations.
`operator=(Parent& other) is typically used for move semantics` give me an exmaple where `operator=(Parent& other) ` is suited
difference between `Parent(Parent&& other)` and `Parent(Parent& other)`
Parent(Parent& other) don't have const before `Parent& other` why is it still copy constructor

You are correct that the signature `Parent(Parent& other)` should technically be a non-const reference and is not a valid copy constructor signature. The correct signature for a copy constructor is `Parent(const Parent& other)`.

Thank you for bringing this to my attention. It was an oversight on my part. I apologize for any confusion it may have caused.
under what scenario do i use `Parent(Parent& other)`, or should i never use it
i have a child class, Child have an extra m_child_prop member that is not in Parent class, do i need to overload copy constructor and operator= for child class?
cmake add_subdirectory

`add_subdirectory()` is a CMake command used to add a subdirectory to the current CMakeLists.txt file. This is useful when you have a complex project with multiple subdirectories and CMakeLists.txt files.

The syntax for `add_subdirectory()` command is:

```
add_subdirectory(<directory> [binary_dir])
```

- `<directory>` is the path to the subdirectory that contains another `CMakeLists.txt` file that you want to include in your project.
- `[binary_dir]` is an optional argument that specifies the directory where CMake will generate build files for the subdirectory. If the argument is not specified, CMake will create build files in the subdirectory itself.

Here's an example of how to use `add_subdirectory()` command in a project structure where there is a subdirectory `"lib"` that contains the source code for a library:

```
project(my_project)

# Include the library directory
add_subdirectory(lib)

# Main executable
add_executable(my_app main.cpp)

# Link the library to the main executable
target_link_libraries(my_app my_lib)
```

In this example, we first use `add_subdirectory(lib)` to include the `"lib"` subdirectory that has its own `CMakeLists.txt` and source files. This command will configure the library object file and make it available to use in our main executable.

Then we create a main executable using `add_executable(my_app main.cpp)` and link it to the library using `target_link_libraries(my_app my_lib)`. The library name `"my_lib"` is the name of the library object that we defined in the `lib/CMakeLists.txt` file.

Using `add_subdirectory()` lets us build our project with multiple directories and dependencies without having to manually handle build settings for each subdirectory.
